Long ago, before the dawn of humanity, the Moon was a flawless sphere of polished silver, forged by Lumis, the God of the Night Sky. His light was so piercingly bright that it rivaled the sun, turning midnight into a pale, restless twilight. The innocent creatures of the earth grew weary, unable to find the dark embrace needed for sleep. 

Seeing their exhaustion, Vespera, the Weaver of Shadows, decided to intervene. Seeking balance, she took the darkest threads of the cosmos and spun them into a swarm of ethereal, velvet-winged Star-Moths. She released them into the sky, commanding them to soften Lumis’s overwhelming glare. 

Without hesitation, the Star-Moths fluttered upward, landing gently on the Moon's pristine surface. Wherever they rested, their shadowy wings absorbed the silver light, leaving behind deep, darkened hollows. Lumis, though at first furious, soon saw the wild earth finally resting peacefully far below. He understood the necessity of Vespera’s gift. 

To this day, the Moon remains scarred with the craters left by the Star-Moths. It no longer shines with blinding perfection, but rather glows with a gentle, dappled luminescence, offering just enough light to guide the lost, and just enough shadow to let the weary dream.

Before time had a name, the great mountains were silent. No cold wind whispered through the tall pines, and no stones rattled down the slopes. In this quiet world lived Aella, a spirit of the morning mist. Aella possessed a voice of unparalleled beauty, yet she wept, for there was no one to hear her songs. Her silver tears formed the first morning dew.

Caelus, the ancient god of stone and earth, awoke to the sound of her sorrow. Moved by her melancholic melody, he decided to grant her an eternal audience. He struck the great canyon walls with his obsidian staff, fracturing the smooth stone and creating deep, hollow caverns. 

"Sing, little mist," Caelus rumbled, a sound like grinding boulders. 

Aella let out a single, tentative note. A moment later, the canyon walls sang it back to her. She laughed loudly and sang a joyful chorus, and the mountains repeated her joy, multiplying her voice until the entire valley hummed with life. 

To this very day, Aella wanders the high peaks. Whenever you call out into a deep canyon, it is not the rock that answers, but Aella, eager to share her eternal song with anyone who will listen.

Myth: Before time was measured, the great mountains stood silent and the skies above them were heavy and still. Aella, a spirit of the morning mist, wandered the peaks, weeping because her beautiful voice was stifled by the dense, unmoving air. Her tears fell as the first silver morning dew.

Caelus, the ancient god of stone and earth, grew weary of her sorrow. Wishing to grant her a stage, he didn't just strike the canyons; he reached up and shattered the heavy sky with his obsidian staff, creating deep cracks that glowed with sudden, brilliant light—the first lightning. 

"Sing now, little mist," Caelus rumbled, his voice echoing as the first crack of thunder. 

Encouraged by the vast, open sky, Aella took a deep breath and sang. Her voice rushed through the shattered firmament, transforming into the fierce, whistling winds. She danced among the jagged peaks, her song sweeping away the breathless stillness to bring the first great storm. 

Today, when the sky darkens and the thunder rolls, it is Caelus striking his staff, inviting Aella to sing. The howling wind that follows is her eternal melody, finally free to rush across the wild earth.

When the piercing drops of starlight breached her waters, Thalassa marveled at their brilliance. She knew her children of the deep had never seen the beauty of the celestial sky above. Rather than hoarding the fallen light, she caught the crystallized tears and crushed them between her palms, grinding them into a fine, glowing dust.

With a sweeping gesture of her tide-shaping hands, Thalassa scattered the radiant dust into the ocean’s deepest currents. The starlight clung to the bells of wandering jellyfish, the scales of abyssal fish, and the sprawling coral reefs. It pulsed with a rhythmic, ethereal glow, mirroring the twinkling constellations above.

Lumis, looking down from the heavens, was amazed to see a reflection of his own sky glowing in the ocean’s depths. To this day, bioluminescence lights the darkest waters, a gift of fallen stars.
